briefing.opening: Hello everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 15, 2026. Today, we will provide in-depth updates on domestic and international efforts to address climate change and key related issues.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Awareness and Response Week Activities
sections.content: Next week, 'UNFCCC Climate Week' is scheduled to be held in Yeosu [1, 20], which is expected to serve as a platform for international discussions on climate change response. Various local governments across the country are operating 'Climate Change Week,' focusing on spreading carbon neutrality practices through lights-out events and citizen experiential activities [3, 4, 6, 7, 10, 12, 13, 15, 16, 18, 23, 24, 25, 26, 30, 81, 83, 88]. In particular, the Jeonnam Provincial Office of Education Training Institute is operating climate change response and ecological transition education programs to strengthen the climate crisis capabilities of future generations [14, 21, 22].

sections.title: ⚠️ Deepening Climate Crisis and Response Funding Gap
sections.content: As climate change leads to earlier and longer summers, warnings have been issued regarding the urgent need for heatwave countermeasures for energy-vulnerable populations [5]. Gwanak-gu has established its '3rd Climate Crisis Adaptation Plan' and plans to implement 44 tasks over five years to enhance resilience to this materialized climate crisis [27, 28, 31, 35]. However, concerns have been raised that domestic climate finance faces an annual shortfall of 20 trillion KRW, with an allocation rate of only 74.2% compared to targets, underscoring the growing need to secure funding for climate crisis response [39].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Efforts Towards a Carbon-Neutral Society
sections.content: Gwangmyeong City is accelerating the realization of carbon neutrality by 2050 through public-private cooperation [33, 49, 68, 72], and We Make Great Stories, the operator of Yogiyo, has also declared carbon neutrality by 2050, expanding reusable container deliveries and reducing single-use items [41, 42, 43, 62, 71, 79, 85]. Meanwhile, Gimhae City is attempting a unique approach by soliciting citizens' 'failed experiences' in practicing carbon neutrality to utilize them for policy improvement [44, 61, 66, 75], and POSCO views carbon neutrality as 'survival, not an option,' strengthening decarbonization solidarity with global steel companies, indicating active movements within the industry [69].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Renewable Energy Expansion Policies and Technological Innovation
sections.content: The government plans to promote the creation of 'Mega Special Zones' encompassing robotics, renewable energy, bio-tech, and AI autonomous vehicles, focusing on deregulation and investment [97, 99, 111, 115]. Specifically, in renewable energy special zones, regulations have been eased to allow direct buying and selling of electricity (renewable energy), and with intensifying power competition in the AI era, Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) are emerging as a means to secure renewable energy [91, 92]. Internationally, the German government is continuing practical efforts to expand renewable energy deployment, such as providing incentives for installing Energy Storage Systems (ESS) in new solar power plants and promoting mandatory battery integration [106, 109].

sections.title: 🌍 Global Climate Change Awareness and International Response Challenges
sections.content: According to a Gallup survey, 44% of Americans express 'concern' about climate change, marking an all-time high, indicating that international awareness of climate change is approaching its peak [11, 118]. However, it has been pointed out that seeking 'technological solutions' to climate change carries many risks, and the opinion has also been raised that reflection is needed on whether radical humility can save the planet [119]. Research findings that African forests are no longer helping to combat climate change and have turned into net carbon emitters [129], along with a report that more than half of British butterfly species have declined [134], once again highlight the severity of climate change and emphasize the urgency of global climate action [133, 135].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the active domestic and international efforts to address climate change, alongside the crises and financial challenges we face. We hope this information provides crucial insights for our efforts to overcome the climate crisis. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Warmer weather has benefited some species in Britain, but others that rely on specific plants or habitats have struggled“Insectageddon” has not occurred, but there has been a loss of butterfly diversity over the past half a century, according to the world’s largest insect monitoring scheme.More than 44m butterfly sightings scientifically collected in Britain since 1976 show that of the 58 native species recorded, 33 species have declined and 25 have increased in number. Continue reading...
